clean_body: Friend, I promise I won't take up much of your time. In my film 2000 Mules , I exposed one of the greatest scandals in American history - the hijacking of the 2020 presidential election. Today, I'm proud to join forces with Stand in the Gap, a non-profit organization dedicated to supporting the patriots who exercised their First Amendment rights in protest of the outcome of that election. I want to reach as many people as I can with this request... I told their founder, Shane Jenkins, that I would help him raise $20,000 before the end of the month to help Stand in the Gap provide help, hope, and healing to the January 6th patriots. Even a donation of $10 can give a January 6th patriot the support they need. Otherwise, let me explain why I 'm asking you to donate at least $10: $10 because the " crime" these patriots committed was fighting for their country. $10 because even though they've been pardoned, many of them are struggling to get back on their feet. $10 because Stand in the Gap is providing them with the resources they need to successfully reintegrate into their communities, find work, reconnect with family, and restore their rights. Not only are these men and women patriots, but they come from all walks of life -- law enforcement, veterans, white-collar professionals, blue-collar workers, and all races and religions. And they were hunted down in a political persecution that denied them their constitutional rights. Stand in the Gap exists to provide real, practical help, like rent money, job training, transportation, legal referrals, trauma counseling, and health insurance, to help these persecuted patriots move on with their lives. Your gift today can help one of these patriots find the help, hope, and healing they need to succeed in their second chance. Friend, can I count on you to contribute $10 or whatever you can afford to Stand in the Gap? Click any button below to give today. Thank you, Dinesh D'Souza P.S. If you're still reading this email and I haven't convinced you to donate $10 or more, please consider what January 6th patriots still face... Harassment on social media and in their personal lives Difficulties in finding a job Homelessness Loss of rights, like the right to vote or own a firearm * Mental health challenges that their families can never understand All they did was stand up for their beliefs. They do not deserve this sort of treatment. But for just $10, you can help Stand in the Gap give pardoned January 6th patriots the support they need to succeed at their second chance. Stand In The Gap is registered as a 501(c)(3) non-profit organization. All contributions are tax-deductible to the extent permitted by law. Stand In The Gap's tax identification number is 93-1417599.

name: Senator Lashrecse Aird
subject: August Newsletter 2025
disclaimer_text: Paid for and authorized by Friends of Lashrecse Aird
body:
logo [https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/Newsletter/Screen%20Shot%202023-11-12%20at%207.15.37%20PM.png]
2025 August Newsletter
Join me on August 27, from 5:30–7:00 PM at the Hopewell High School Library, in partnership with the Hopewell-Petersburg Community Action Network, a subsidiary of the City of Hopewell’s Department of Healthy Families. The event, “New Laws Unlocked: Community Conversations on Change,” will provide updates on new laws taking effect July 1, the impact of recent federal actions, and how ICE agreements affect local communities.
Given recent federal actions, the responsibility has never been greater for state governments to uphold our shared values and protect our communities. This town hall is about more than policy updates, but also ensuring Virginians understand the laws shaping their lives and know that their voices are essential in helping to build the solutions that reflect those values. Register and submit your questions here. [https://www.lashrecseaird.com/8-27-25]
https://www.lashrecseaird.com/8-27-25 [https://www.lashrecseaird.com/8-27-25]
https://www.facebook.com/airdcampaign [https://www.facebook.com/airdcampaign] https://twitter.com/lashrecseaird [https://twitter.com/lashrecseaird] https://www.instagram.com/lashrecseaird/ [https://www.instagram.com/lashrecseaird/]
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/Newsletter/Untitled%20design%20%2831%29.png] Protecting Virginia's Values in a Time of Federal Uncertainty
In this moment of deep political division and uncertainty, the role of state government has never been more important. That is why I believe so deeply in the responsibility we have in Richmond to use our authority to protect the rights, opportunities, and institutions that allow all Virginians to thrive. This year, we saw an attempt by the Governor to stack the Boards of Visitors at our universities with appointees advancing an anti-diversity agenda, part of a larger pattern of aligning our Commonwealth with the Trump administration’s regressive policies. I stood alongside Senate leadership, former Governor Doug Wilder, and Democratic colleagues to block these appointments — and a court has now i [https://virginiamercury.com/2025/07/29/judge-temporarily-suspends-appointees-senators-rejected-from-serving-on-college-governing-boards/] ssued a preliminary injunction [https://virginiamercury.com/2025/07/29/judge-temporarily-suspends-appointees-senators-rejected-from-serving-on-college-governing-boards/] [https://virginiamercury.com/2025/07/29/judge-temporarily-suspends-appointees-senators-rejected-from-serving-on-college-governing-boards/] preventing these individuals from serving until the General Assembly confirms them.
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/2025/2025%20August%20Newsletter/IMG_4675.JPG]
These actions are not just about defending higher education; they are about defending the principle that public institutions should be bastions of inclusion, free thought, and opportunity — not tools for political ideology. At a time when our national politics are fraying the threads that hold our communities together, I remain committed to strengthening those bonds here at home, ensuring that Virginia is a place where everyone belongs and can succeed.
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/Newsletter/Untitled%20design%20%2830%29.png] Commissions Spotlight
While the legislature is out of session, that does not mean the work slows down. I am proud to serve as Vice Chair of two important statewide commissions — roles that allow me to influence major policy areas critical to Virginia’s future.
Tobacco Region Revitalization Commission
This commission invests in economic development, education, and infrastructure projects in communities historically dependent on tobacco farming. As Vice Chair, I help set funding priorities, evaluate project proposals, and ensure that investments address both immediate needs and long-term growth. My past service as Vice Chair to the Commission while a member of the House of Delegates has given me valuable experience in how to direct resources toward projects that create jobs, expand broadband, and open new doors for rural and economically distressed areas. Learn more about the commission here. [https://revitalizeva.org/about-the-commission/]
https://revitalizeva.org/about-the-commission/ [https://revitalizeva.org/about-the-commission/]
Joint Commission on a Cannabis Retail Marketplace
This newly formed commission is tasked with developing a comprehensive legal framework for retail cannabis sales in Virginia. As Vice Chair, I help lead hearings, guide stakeholder engagement, and shape recommendations for legislation that ensures a safe, equitable, and sustainable market. This includes improving the medical cannabis program, promoting economic opportunities for small and minority-owned businesses, and ensuring revenues are invested in community reinvestment and early childhood education. I believe strongly that the final plan must work for all Virginians — moving cannabis sales from the street corner to a regulated, age-verified retail space. Read more about the Commission in the news. [https://virginiamercury.com/2025/07/09/joint-cannabis-commission-holds-first-meeting-amid-push-for-regulated-market/]
https://virginiamercury.com/2025/07/09/joint-cannabis-commission-holds-first-meeting-amid-push-for-regulated-market/ [https://virginiamercury.com/2025/07/09/joint-cannabis-commission-holds-first-meeting-amid-push-for-regulated-market/]
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/Newsletter/Untitled%20design%20%2833%29.png] Reporting Flood Damage
If you experienced property damage or other issues from the recent flooding in Petersburg, please report it to EM@petersburg-va.org [EM@petersburg-va.org] . Your information will be forwarded to the appropriate department so the City can respond and track the full impact of this event.
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/Newsletter/Untitled%20design%20%2833%29.png] Farewell to Dani Girardi
This summer, we say goodbye to District Director Dani Girardi. Dani first joined my team as a field organizer during my primary campaign, later became campaign manager for my general election, and ultimately served as District Director for my legislative office. Her commitment, skill, and heart for the community have been an incredible asset to our work. I’ve been honored to watch her grow in each role, and I could not be more proud of all she has accomplished. I know she will continue to make a positive impact as she begins the next chapter of her journey.
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/2025/2025%20August%20Newsletter/Dani%20Collage-min.png]
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/Newsletter/Untitled%20design%20%2831%29.png] Around the District
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/August%20Collage-min.png]
* 250th Anniversary Ball for the Quartermaster Corps – Ft. Gregg-Adams

During the 250th Anniversary was honored to present Senate Resolution 670 commemorating the 250th anniversary of the U.S. Army Quartermaster Corps — a branch that has quietly but critically ensured our military’s readiness since the nation’s earliest days. Quartermasters are responsible for supplying everything from food and fuel to uniforms and equipment, sustaining the force at home and abroad. This anniversary celebration paid tribute to generations of service members who have fulfilled this mission with precision, ingenuity, and dedication, and it was a privilege to join them in honoring that proud legacy.

* Top 40 Under 40 – Southern Virginia Regional Chamber of Commerce Prince George

Being recognized among this year’s “Top 40 Under 40” honorees was a humbling and inspiring moment. The award highlights emerging leaders from across Southern Virginia who are making a measurable difference in business, civic engagement, and community development. For me, it’s a reminder that leadership is not about titles but about service — building opportunities, creating connections, and ensuring that progress reaches every corner of our region. I am grateful to stand alongside so many talented individuals committed to shaping a brighter future for all.

* Bruce Tucker Historical Marker Unveiling – Dinwiddie County

Joining community members for the unveiling of the historical marker honoring Bruce Oliver Tucker, a Dinwiddie native whose tragic death in 1968 became a pivotal moment in the history of medical ethics and civil rights, was truly an impactful event. Without his family’s consent, Mr. Tucker’s heart was transplanted into another patient — one of the first human heart transplants in the U.S. His case exposed deep racial inequities in the health care system and helped spark national conversations about informed consent and patient rights. Recognizing his story through this marker ensures that his life, and the lessons learned from it, are never forgotten. Read more here. [https://www.vcuhealth.org/news/historical-highway-marker-commemorating-bruce-oliver-tucker-unveiled-in-his-hometown/]

* National Farmers Market Week – River Street Market, Petersburg
National Farmers Market Week gave us the chance to celebrate not just fresh produce, but the community connections that markets like River Street foster every week. Farmers markets strengthen local economies, support small businesses, and ensure families have access to healthy, affordable food. I was especially proud to meet Arianna, the young owner of Pickles Pastries LLC, who is proving that entrepreneurship has no age limit. Her example shows other young people that they don’t have to wait to start making their mark — and that our community is stronger when we support and invest in local talent.
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/Newsletter/Untitled%20design%20%2833%29.png] 2026 Senate Page Program Applications Open!
The Senate Page Program is one of the most unique opportunities available to Virginia’s young leaders — a tradition dating back to 1850. Each year, 40 students from across the Commonwealth are selected to serve as Pages in the Virginia Senate during the General Assembly session.
Pages learn firsthand how laws are made, develop professional skills through a structured work environment, and gain insight into civic life that will serve them for years to come. Applicants must be 13 or 14 years old on the first day of session, and applications are due by 5:00 PM on Wednesday, October 1. If you know a motivated young person ready to step into this incredible role, please encourage them to apply. More information is available here. [https://sites.google.com/senate.virginia.gov/prospectivesenatepageportal/home]
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP21/1/89018/images/2025/2025%20August%20Newsletter/Page%20Photo.png]
As always, my team and I are here to support you. During the legislative interim, we’re continuing district outreach, preparing for the next session, and advocating for your needs every step of the way. Reach us anytime at:
📞 (804) 452-7076
📧 senatoraird@senate.virginia.gov
🌐 www.lashrecseaird.com [http://www.lashrecseaird.com]
DONATE [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/la_website_direct]
https://www.facebook.com/airdcampaign [https://www.facebook.com/airdcampaign] https://twitter.com/lashrecseaird [https://twitter.com/lashrecseaird] https://www.instagram.com/lashrecseaird/ [https://www.instagram.com/lashrecseaird/]

Petersburg, VA 23805

Petersburg, VA 23805

name: Jason Miyares
subject: This racist attack on Winsome Sears is unacceptable
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Miyares for Virginia |
body:
Friend, enough is enough.| | | Friend,
---
[](http://links.jasonforvirginia.com/s/c/Aomoc5bBlsN98UwDti52ce9IgiHgSOk9d7nLPzbrHxW-5sq2xix1nnnuldSvgX4LtB-9GlGrA1s6SXZ8qRf111D3VYbiRvc9pIE8G8yjVIOgfcadCi6b0XbBFIFVffn55R7AQGzyX3FQtjD3l3FMGNwUws7muy1ajwYGpZQSm59ALcOk6i8ugDil-8hNis3nbKQUOgSpfFazwuTArSjSoCpesFnwVZ_jeqa6l4Z8h4ZZTiS2ySRHgOPWZJJ6lsLsSrnQ5RSPn2uglPIPn_cjMVqJORXYjg7lkTKSSQWK7xtvAWMt5UPKTEqyZ9Cc68Y_t5lDbqUB-Uem0NXttxbxagVmEn5qsAbOjQNeHVh1AcGEzXeHDQWpxB0uFKD67mQQLkdKi_0xqFTToUd8XCtsp3ICg9E1dSLjbq2b0mOnYgUHvsrR3YPkFDqM75OYnuKKF6OnP_4Mn2oq7RH2yYwa879N3cMhnTYzcclWT4wo2VLSf-CNHer56dgwCCpFzV44V59gvwSAgQUGOwHB3TXkwUehNFL_kUEdWV2NnLFfeJjzUnqMn7V-5UEtyTS275Qr9F5jsQ-87LPrA3VIWFVb/jkjdMdmbJXo5hV4ibkxrH9C1tj54zeyK/12)
---
_A protester holds a sign reading “Hey Winsome, if trans can't share your bathroom, then**blacks** can’t share my water fountain.”_
---
Earlier this summer, my radical opponent Jay Jones said of me: "You know him, you hate him — he's a right-wing extremist" — **not even trying to hide the hatred the Left has for anyone who dares oppose their radical agenda.**

Then yesterday, a proud Democrat volunteer displayed **this racist sign** at the Arlington school board meeting where **LG Winsome Earle-Sears and parents were standing up for Virginia's girls** — demanding they protect our daughters from radical bathroom policies.

This is who the Democrats actually are.

This was nasty. This was vile. **And Virginia Democrats have been slow to condemn it.**
---
[ **HELP ME HOLD THEM ACCOUNTABLE** ](http://links.jasonforvirginia.com/s/c/iNMQISmLWc0niGmk_Vii5owOXyL5Lmx8NGuzBvuC3hEzWwx6k1KGn5B-fiDekprgQ85FJ-AnYoP-pKNPLDzEC99l2IDrMLnY8gVjuQG-bhxSUqS3-P4Ag71dW_qr0U3Ycgv8R-m4q25H0u8ikUaHgcHZmA0YunuMbn9dJB9k-lpqlVlNM8U5ikxdjJPFM61ShNMsur4DDa9npgS9Km8z7-DAwg0QiYz-GrDNLJpfX-t4rlECFavBQiTi1dSLsj-WN9IjyVxDO9HWwIFPIJkf06X0hD1Y8jQwG2STqk0xS8e3_oTJkvduj-FqzvJPNJ2bHY-EJ6kmPTQbIisZ_Z6Q3C2i8SB6bhutWL-OhT0wr71qC6R0wKAppUbzlNPsScgAzkntpwMGz7VeyHnZBJWAQNzyJ2F2LhTB2da9fOWRos6J1Cxdv3g5TTey50mHSa78nV2hfl-5-0bjRWaCGDjhYYtYPVcRH_mMncB5CE67EqME-lqtTrX-YrVS5t7VRcL08IHpK5Fi0D0efXx0YQ_CXHr3tQ10lZsHWR3I8gWRKd2CEzW_UCe69LGCYbiFmn8ltWru_ipWStcw7zSJqiZF/mqKutbL1uTQb5L9nmplhH98zo7iwj2yu/12)
---
Protecting girls’ privacy and safety is common sense; it should not invite Jim Crow-style attacks.

**Enough is enough.** We cannot allow their agenda of hate to win in Virginia.

Protecting our daughters, keeping violent criminals behind bars, and ensuring that parents have the final say in their kids’ education are common-sense positions we all share. **The Democrats oppose all of it, and we cannot let them succeed.**

**And as your AG, I'll keep fighting — no matter how ugly and hate-filled their attacks get.**

[_**Will you stand with me and help hold them accountable in November?**_](http://links.jasonforvirginia.com/s/c/ygjDV3RhBqL3Bugg07SI8cJP7nB9PLOd46qyQ_u3ZwQ-kirBxpW_TMO-DTICeltzok4RMhtRy8UJqmqa_Au9PUHKQEEQq3F47uLtd5w1L1ywZbgq30owjxQsA3x3fcUr4oqz9o9UzLl33DlJfM3nEa-NxlWkbXvXYsCLiQwwXCEogOQDoy4z3Im5-wKyuSfxQaborEdVQejanh6y_ZBYS2jJsPSAwFHyCIBWmEf6RoBS73NFlQSMkG3UWn75rdktohEPTQqFKOi4GgBe3C-O2K402jbV08Go8pEatMiRHwjkrgReZLb1NkOhFOuRAypZFyTSwU3yTQ0wF6wHfyLrdxinSo1eefk-U2ENqZY6sZB1vIZUbBLPZ7U5AbSgrta6YJyjSHOq1hRojxCf4vjMwgTx-AASZRUcpJ1zjkrFHjvFmfDt1xWCAs650Rj9VP5aL7qZO5k70ijdmsbIVjNYAU-F8mImeYBQdhv7R5bLOakFxX-twRIm_4_wGFqHzJ6bN3SbJ1-vAeMbxgWFjPUC6JwFl0eYu0oMDL6r8qdf_Yue4pxz2e-IiNoiBCz5apO8AAw4ue5iT2rXs-lBUTmA/eEGoEMUDGFlAQ8b86zePrOzFYyjfWNMF/12)

clean_body: Friend, enough is enough.| | | Friend, A protester holds a sign reading “Hey Winsome, if trans can't share your bathroom, thenblacks can’t share my water fountain.” Earlier this summer, my radical opponent Jay Jones said of me: "You know him, you hate him — he's a right-wing extremist" — not even trying to hide the hatred the Left has for anyone who dares oppose their radical agenda. Then yesterday, a proud Democrat volunteer displayed this racist sign at the Arlington school board meeting where LG Winsome Earle-Sears and parents were standing up for Virginia's girls — demanding they protect our daughters from radical bathroom policies. This is who the Democrats actually are. This was nasty. This was vile. And Virginia Democrats have been slow to condemn it. Protecting girls’ privacy and safety is common sense; it should not invite Jim Crow-style attacks. Enough is enough. We cannot allow their agenda of hate to win in Virginia. Protecting our daughters, keeping violent criminals behind bars, and ensuring that parents have the final say in their kids’ education are common-sense positions we all share. The Democrats oppose all of it, and we cannot let them succeed. And as your AG, I'll keep fighting — no matter how ugly and hate-filled their attacks get. Peter,
Not even 18 months ago, Republicans were screaming that if Joe Biden ever tried to federalize a state’s National Guard, it would be a direct attack on states’ rights .
They said Democrats were trying to take away freedoms of religion, assembly, and speech.
Well, that didn’t age well...
This week, four GOP governors announced they’re sending their National Guard troops into Washington, D.C., to help Donald Trump occupy the nation’s capital.
Violent crime in D.C. actually dropped 26% last year. But the White House is boasting that in just 15 days into this occupation, 225+ and counting “illegal aliens” were arrested, 91 firearms seized, and 719 people arrested.
This isn’t about safety. It’s about Trump tightening his grip on power, scapegoating vulnerable communities, and normalizing military control of American streets.
Congress is moving to respond, but Trump is moving faster. That’s why we can’t wait . Movement for Freedom is actively on the ground, mobilizing legal defense, rapid response, and community safety teams to resist his takeover. [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/upl_m4f_email-25q3?refcode=x_250822_DCarrests_act]

clean_body: [] Peter, Not even 18 months ago, Republicans were screaming that if Joe Biden ever tried to federalize a state’s National Guard, it would be a direct attack on states’ rights . They said Democrats were trying to take away freedoms of religion, assembly, and speech. Well, that didn’t age well... This week, four GOP governors announced they’re sending their National Guard troops into Washington, D.C., to help Donald Trump occupy the nation’s capital. Violent crime in D.C. actually dropped 26% last year. But the White House is boasting that in just 15 days into this occupation, 225+ and counting “illegal aliens” were arrested, 91 firearms seized, and 719 people arrested. This isn’t about safety. It’s about Trump tightening his grip on power, scapegoating vulnerable communities, and normalizing military control of American streets. Congress is moving to respond, but Trump is moving faster. That’s why we can’t wait . Movement for Freedom is actively on the ground, mobilizing legal defense, rapid response, and community safety teams to resist his takeover. [] This is quite literally the fight of our lives, and it’s not just about D.C. If Trump can occupy the capital, he can occupy any city in America. Chip in now to help us resist Trump’s authoritarian power grab >> [] If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will go through immediately: DONATE $5 [] DONATE $10 [] DONATE $25 [] DONATE $50 [] DONATE $100 [] OTHER [] In solidarity, — Movement for Freedom [] Movement for Freedom is a campaign, founded by former Congresswoman Cori Bush, that calls for radically transforming how we build justice, safety, and liberation through love and solidarity. Taking away long held VA workers’ rights is a direct attack on working people and will do nothing to improve veterans’ care.** I proudly served this country in uniform, so I refuse to sit by while Trump and his administration target veterans and workers who care for us. I refuse to sit by while they put the benefits we earned on the chopping block. [**I’m fighting back against this union-busting and working to protect veterans and VA workers. Western Pennsylvanians have already seen how Trump’s attacks on the VA have hurt our veterans. We’ve seen reports that his hiring freeze affected life-saving VA clinical trials and medical research happening right here.
Now, Trump’s union-busting the hard working men and women who serve my fellow veterans—putting the benefits we earned at risk once more.
_It’s a betrayal of my fellow veterans._
We wrote a blank check when we stepped up to serve our country, and we earned the best VA possible.
[**That’s why I’m fighting back to defend the care my fellow veterans earned and protect the rights of the hard working people at the VA—and I need you to stand with me. clean_body: VA terminates union contracts, thousands of Pennsylvania workers affected Earlier this month, the Trump administration stripped thousands of unionized VA workers in Pennsylvania of labor protections, as part of its plan to terminate collective bargaining agreements with federal unions. Derek, make no mistake: unions built the middle class. Taking away long held VA workers’ rights is a direct attack on working people and will do nothing to improve veterans’ care. I proudly served this country in uniform, so I refuse to sit by while Trump and his administration target veterans and workers who care for us. I refuse to sit by while they put the benefits we earned on the chopping block. = | | = Western Pennsylvanians have already seen how Trump’s attacks on the VA have hurt our veterans. We’ve seen reports that his hiring freeze affected life-saving VA clinical trials and medical research happening right here. Now, Trump’s union-busting the hard working men and women who serve my fellow veterans—putting the benefits we earned at risk once more. It’s a betrayal of my fellow veterans. We wrote a blank check when we stepped up to serve our country, and we earned the best VA possible. = If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will go through immediately: | | | = | = | | | = | = | | | = | = Thanks for your support, Chris | Make sure this email goes to your inbox. If every supporter reading this donated a 3X-matched gift of $7 to support state Dems today, we’d have the resources to defend and expand Democratic power from right-wing extremists in state legislatures across the map. 💪 But we know not everyone can pitch in right now, so ahead of our August deadline, we’re relying on our most dedicated supporters to make your first donation of the year to the DLCC. name: Sharice Davids
subject: I’m tired of playing defense all the time
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY SHARICE FOR CONGRESS
body:
I think a lot of Kansas Democrats (including myself) have this on our minds right now

[](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113809062/565011629/-240735101?refcode=email-header&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDIvMS82NTc1OCIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIxNjJjMDg2NS04NzdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=gM00p96mEikfXOcm3gG_lpDvl7tzCA5G_LFt3KI-aqQ=) I’m sorry for my role in your overflowing email inbox, but this note isn’t just to ask for your support. It’s also to say something I believe a lot of Kansas Democrats (including myself) have on our minds right now: **As a Democrat, I don’t want to play defense anymore. I want to play on offense. And I’m willing to bet you feel the same.** [_**I ran for Congress to pass good policies. Not just vote against bad ones. But if we want to make that a reality, we have to do everything in our power to flip the House blue next year — which starts with holding competitive seats like this one. So if you’re just as ready as I am for Democrats to go on offense, will you chip in to support Team Sharice?**_](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113809063/565011630/212706120?refcode=email_082225_defense&amounts=5,25,50,100,250,500,1000&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDIvMS82NTc1OCIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIxNjJjMDg2NS04NzdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=gM00p96mEikfXOcm3gG_lpDvl7tzCA5G_LFt3KI-aqQ=) | | [Donate →](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113809064/565011631/212706120?refcode=email_082225_defense&amounts=5,25,50,100,250,500,1000&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDIvMS82NTc1OCIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIxNjJjMDg2NS04NzdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=gM00p96mEikfXOcm3gG_lpDvl7tzCA5G_LFt3KI-aqQ=)

Listen, I fully understand that playing defense is my job right now. With Donald Trump taking a sledgehammer to our communities, our livelihoods, and the Constitution itself, we have to fight with everything we’ve got to protect families from the National GOP’s chaos and cruelty.
But I think you’ll agree with me on this:**Preventing _bad_ things doesn’t compare to accomplishing _good_ things.**
If we flip control of Congress next year, we can begin the work to pass _good_ legislation in Congress — legislation to lower costs, create jobs, confront the climate crisis, tackle corruption, and so much more.
I want to play on offense. And with your help, we can do it — and finally start putting points on the board for working families all across Kansas.
[_**If you’re with me, I’d be so grateful to have your support ahead of our next fundraising deadline. Because there’s so much work we need to get done for Kansas and our country, and holding this competitive seat is the only way we can do it.**_](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113809065/565011632/212706120?refcode=email_082225_defense&amounts=5,25,50,100,250,500,1000&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDIvMS82NTc1OCIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIxNjJjMDg2NS04NzdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=gM00p96mEikfXOcm3gG_lpDvl7tzCA5G_LFt3KI-aqQ=)

body:
When you love this country, you fight for the people who make it work.
[](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113809609/565012276/-529377601?amount=10&refcode=LOC_EM_FR_2025.08.22_Sherrod-Brown-Split_B1_F1_S1_C1_45AC_N-T0&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTkvMS83MjcyMiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI0MzZiODMxYi04ODdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=nMvd0erO2raKsnxzfhd9A7ez_I7YNmysViJhrmZdk0U=) When you love this country, you fight for the people who make it work. These words have guided my life in public service. So it pains me to see everyday folks working harder than ever but still falling behind – while D.C. politicians give handout after handout to their wealthy donors and corporate special interest friends. **That’s why I’m getting back in this fight. **[**_Will you chip in $10 or more now – split between Kirsten Gillibrand and me – to help fuel our grassroots movement and flip Ohio’s Senate seat?_**](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113809610/565012277/-529377601?amount=10&refcode=LOC_EM_FR_2025.08.22_Sherrod-Brown-Split_B1_F1_S1_C1_45AC_N-T0&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTkvMS83MjcyMiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI0MzZiODMxYi04ODdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=nMvd0erO2raKsnxzfhd9A7ez_I7YNmysViJhrmZdk0U=) _If you've saved payment information with ActBlue Express, your split donation to Sherrod Brown and Kirsten Gillibrand will go through immediately:_ | | | [CHIP IN $10 >>](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113809611/565012278/-529377601?express_lane=true&amount=10&refcode=LOC_EM_FR_2025.08.22_Sherrod-Brown-Split_B1_F1_S1_C1_45AC_N-T0&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTkvMS83MjcyMiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI0MzZiODMxYi04ODdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=nMvd0erO2raKsnxzfhd9A7ez_I7YNmysViJhrmZdk0U=)

---
I never planned to run for office again. But I see what’s happening in Washington, and I can’t sit on the sidelines. Right now, no one in the Senate seems to be working for the people of Ohio, and that’s a job that I know I can do.
This isn’t about left versus right. It’s about who you fight for – and who you’re willing to stand up to.
When we fought to save the pensions of tens-of-thousands of Ohio retirees, we stood up to Wall Street and won. When we fought to cap insulin prices and bring down the cost of prescription drugs, we stood up to Big Pharma and _won_.
Together, we can build an economy where hard work pays off and where everyone has a shot at a decent life – but I need you in my corner.
**Our Senate race is going to be one of the most competitive races of 2026, and corporate special interests will spend millions of dollars to defeat me. The only way I can fight back and win is with your grassroots support.**
[**_I’m personally asking: if you believe in my vision for a brighter, fairer future for all, will you chip in $10 now – split between my campaign and Kirsten Gillibrand’s – to help me win in Ohio and deliver for workers across the country?_**](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113809619/565012283/-529377601?amount=10&refcode=LOC_EM_FR_2025.08.22_Sherrod-Brown-Split_B1_F1_S1_C1_45AC_N-T0&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTkvMS83MjcyMiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI0MzZiODMxYi04ODdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=nMvd0erO2raKsnxzfhd9A7ez_I7YNmysViJhrmZdk0U=)
Thank you for standing with me.
In solidarity,
Sherrod Brown

clean_body: When you love this country, you fight for the people who make it work. = When you love this country, you fight for the people who make it work. These words have guided my life in public service. So it pains me to see everyday folks working harder than ever but still falling behind – while D.C. politicians give handout after handout to their wealthy donors and corporate special interest friends. That’s why I’m getting back in this fight. = If you've saved payment information with ActBlue Express, your split donation to Sherrod Brown and Kirsten Gillibrand will go through immediately: | | | = | | = | | = | | = | | = I never planned to run for office again. But I see what’s happening in Washington, and I can’t sit on the sidelines. Right now, no one in the Senate seems to be working for the people of Ohio, and that’s a job that I know I can do. This isn’t about left versus right. It’s about who you fight for – and who you’re willing to stand up to. When we fought to save the pensions of tens-of-thousands of Ohio retirees, we stood up to Wall Street and won. When we fought to cap insulin prices and bring down the cost of prescription drugs, we stood up to Big Pharma and won. Together, we can build an economy where hard work pays off and where everyone has a shot at a decent life – but I need you in my corner. Our Senate race is going to be one of the most competitive races of 2026, and corporate special interests will spend millions of dollars to defeat me. The only way I can fight back and win is with your grassroots support. = Thank you for standing with me. In solidarity, Sherrod Brown * =

name: Sara Jacobs
subject: How are you doing?
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Sara Jacobs for Congress
body:
https://secure.actblue.com/donate/sjj-em [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/sjj-em]
Hi friends.
How are you doing?
Not a rhetorical question — I hope you’ll reply and let me know! I love this team, and I like to check in every now and then to hear from you and get a sense of how folks are navigating our current reality.
Anything going on in your life that’s bringing joy, big or small? Any hobbies helping you relax and recharge?
For me, listening to music is always a great escape — and looking forward to the new Taylor Swift album that’s coming out in October is already bringing major joy. (Any other Swifties in this community?!)
However things are going, I’d love to hear from you.
Have a great weekend.
-Sara

name: Amanda Sherman Baity
subject: Let's agree to be done electing mediocre men.
body:
This team is endorsing the right leaders and helping them every step of the way. |
---
[](https://click.everyaction.com/k/113809636/565012300/-140265690?refcode=WUEFE849_20250822_Amanda_ALL_v1s1&sourceid=1056847&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9UU00vVFNNRUwvMS84NzA2OSIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI1MWY4NTkyYS04ODdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=CdlEv5QA0_UXK1stofJciGKIYLE0M41wcH3kvxMR8t8=) Hi Peter,

I'm Amanda Sherman Baity, and I'm thrilled to introduce myself as the new chief communications officer here at EMILYs List.

If there's one thing you should know about me, it's this: I love a tough fight. Over the last decade, I've worked on red-to-blue races, U.S. Senate battles, and congressional showdowns. I've seen what it takes to flip seats and shift power.

I've also seen, time and again, that EMILYs List shows up to do the work. And they make a real impact in tough races. | [DONATE](https://click.everyaction.com/k/113809637/565012301/-140265690?refcode=WUEFE849_20250822_Amanda_ALL_v1s1&sourceid=1056847&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9UU00vVFNNRUwvMS84NzA2OSIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI1MWY4NTkyYS04ODdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=CdlEv5QA0_UXK1stofJciGKIYLE0M41wcH3kvxMR8t8=)
---
EMILYs List jumps in early — backing bold, authentic women candidates and grinding out every phase of the race, shoulder to shoulder with campaigns. That's why, when I had the chance to join this team, I didn't hesitate for a second.

This is the best team in the game. We share the same values and the same mission: electing leaders who will deliver, because great policy starts with electing the right people. And EMILYs List knows that the right people to lead our party and our country into our next chapter are Democratic pro-choice women.

As we look to 2026, we're laser-focused on building a candidate bench that is ready to meet this moment. And let's be honest: Republican attacks are getting nastier, more expensive, and more relentless by the day. I've seen what happens when campaigns aren't ready for that onslaught. EMILYs List can make the difference.

We think ten steps ahead. We fight smart. And we win.

I'll be updating you more as the cycle heats up. But for now, just know this: I'm here to fight back against Republican hypocrisy — and to replace the MAGA extremists who have lied to their voters, failed their constituents, and betrayed their country, time and time again.
****[**So, Peter, will you join me and help power this movement into 2026 and beyond?**](https://click.everyaction.com/k/113809638/565012302/-140265690?refcode=WUEFE849_20250822_Amanda_ALL_v1s1&sourceid=1056847&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9UU00vVFNNRUwvMS84NzA2OSIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI1MWY4NTkyYS04ODdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=CdlEv5QA0_UXK1stofJciGKIYLE0M41wcH3kvxMR8t8=)****

We share the same values and the same mission: electing leaders who will deliver, because great policy starts with electing the right people. As we look to 2026, we're laser-focused on building a candidate bench that is ready to meet this moment. ##August 29, 2005 -- Hurricane Katrina makes landfall
As we approach the 20th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina, I'll be publishing several pieces covering my own perspective, the challenges still creating danger in South Louisiana, and lessons learned (and the ones that weren't).
Please consider upgrading to a paid subscription so I can keep this newsletter free to the world.
[Subscribed](https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubWVzb3NjYWxlbmV3cy5jb20vYWNjb3VudCIsInAiOjE3MTY4MDg2NSwicyI6MjgzNDQ5LCJmIjpmYWxzZSwidSI6OTA1MDExOTIsImlhdCI6MTc1NTg4ODcwMSwiZXhwIjoyMDcxNDY0NzAxLCJpc3MiOiJwdWItMCIsInN1YiI6ImxpbmstcmVkaXJlY3QifQ.AwolHLsI-I4BDiBPsDv__yYp94X34SMmZaxpAnk-6Z4?)
* * *
Very few moments in a lifetime actually shift the axis your world spins on. For me, Katrina was one of them--the storm that rewired how I saw the world, matched only by the day my son was born and when the state raided my home. What I lived through on August 29, 2005, and in the long, bitter weeks that followed, planted in me the conviction that truth is not optional, that someone has to hold it fast. That storm was less a detour than a compass, and it set me on the path I still walk today.
It's hard to wrap my head around the fact that twenty years have passed since the storm. Time says it's history, but it doesn't feel that way--it feels like something still raw and open, a wound that never closed. Katrina didn't just arrive and vanish; it settled in, reshaping lives, communities, the whole country. I was there, and in many ways, I'm still there. The storm lingers, like high water that never quite drains away.
What Katrina revealed was not simply the power of a hurricane, but the fragility of a society that had ignored every warning.
I remember reading the maps years before--New Orleans drawn like a bowl, with walls of dirt between it and the sea, everyone knowing those levees were not built to withstand what the Gulf could send. Engineers and scientists said it plainly.
When the storm came, it did what we knew it would. The failure wasn't natural. It was political. It was social.
And after the waters finally receded, I thought, surely this was the moment the country would wake up. Surely, after watching our fellow citizens stranded on rooftops and left to die in the heat of the Superdome, we would treat poverty and climate as the national emergencies they are. But we didn't. We went back to business as usual. We let leaders call it a freak storm, an anomaly. We convinced ourselves it wouldn't happen again.
South Louisiana still bears the scars. I've walked through neighborhoods where the houses remain gutted, skeletal reminders of promises never kept. I've spoken with families who fled to Houston, Atlanta, Dallas--who wanted to come home but never could. Those who did return often found their communities reshaped not by renewal but by removal. Entire blocks of Black and working-class families were erased, replaced by developers and investors. I can still see the waterlines, faint and high up the walls, as if the city itself keeps a memory of its drowning.
And then the storms kept coming--Rita just weeks later, then Gustav, Ike, Isaac, Ida.
[Subscribed](https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubWVzb3NjYWxlbmV3cy5jb20vYWNjb3VudCIsInAiOjE3MTY4MDg2NSwicyI6MjgzNDQ5LCJmIjpmYWxzZSwidSI6OTA1MDExOTIsImlhdCI6MTc1NTg4ODcwMSwiZXhwIjoyMDcxNDY0NzAxLCJpc3MiOiJwdWItMCIsInN1YiI6ImxpbmstcmVkaXJlY3QifQ.AwolHLsI-I4BDiBPsDv__yYp94X34SMmZaxpAnk-6Z4?)
Each one another hammer on a glass wall already cracked. Every hundred minutes, Louisiana loses a football field of land to the Gulf, wetlands slashed apart by oil and gas canals, the sea reclaiming what human neglect had left defenseless.
But if Louisiana bore the brunt of the levee failures, Mississippi absorbed the storm itself.
The surge there was almost unthinkable -- 28.5 feet high in some places, turning coastal towns into kindling. I drove through Waveland and Bay St. Louis months later, and it looked as if someone had scraped the coastline clean with a giant blade. Whole neighborhoods gone, not just damaged but erased. Casinos that had once floated just offshore were hurled inland like toys, crushing houses beneath them.
Families who had survived generations of hurricanes -- 1969's Camille included --stood stunned in front of nothing but slabs. Biloxi's historic waterfront was shredded, its shrimping fleets splintered, its homes flattened. It's telling that even today, many of those Mississippi towns never truly came back; the tax base was too weak, the resources too few, and the national attention too fixated on New Orleans to sustain long-term rebuilding. Mississippi's pain was quieter, less televised, but no less immense.
And I think often about how little has changed in the systems meant to protect us.
FEMA is still a maze of forms and delays, quicker to redraw insurance maps than to deliver aid. Emergency response still bends toward those with means. And most damning of all, the carbon keeps pouring into the atmosphere.
In the twenty years since Katrina, we've emitted more greenhouse gases than in all of human history before 1990.
We knew -- _we knew_ -- that hotter seas meant stronger hurricanes, that warmer air meant wetter storms, that rising seas meant higher surges. We had the science. We had the evidence. And still we chose to accelerate, to subsidize fossil fuels, to silence the very warnings that might have spared lives.
The media didn't help.
[Subscribed](https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubWVzb3NjYWxlbmV3cy5jb20vYWNjb3VudCIsInAiOjE3MTY4MDg2NSwicyI6MjgzNDQ5LCJmIjpmYWxzZSwidSI6OTA1MDExOTIsImlhdCI6MTc1NTg4ODcwMSwiZXhwIjoyMDcxNDY0NzAxLCJpc3MiOiJwdWItMCIsInN1YiI6ImxpbmstcmVkaXJlY3QifQ.AwolHLsI-I4BDiBPsDv__yYp94X34SMmZaxpAnk-6Z4?)
I remember watching how they framed it: Black families wading through chest-deep water in search of food and medicine were called "looters." White families doing the same were called "survivors."
Reporters descended to marvel at the spectacle of misery but too rarely interrogated the policies that made it inevitable. Mississippi, especially, slipped out of the narrative almost entirely.
The images from New Orleans--the helicopters, the Superdome, the Convention Center--sucked up all the airtime, while the coast to the east was left largely invisible. It was as if an entire state's devastation didn't count because it didn't fit the storyline. That failure hasn't disappeared. To this day, most storm coverage is still framed in radar loops and wind speeds, reporters lashed to poles, drama without depth. Rarely do they connect the dots: poverty makes disasters deadlier, climate change makes them stronger, policy makes them inevitable. Katrina became a symbol of tragedy, but not the catalyst for the kind of transformation it demanded.
And if you look around America in 2025, you see Katrina everywhere. You see it in the wildfires that turn California's skies into a kind of apocalyptic red, in the farmworkers in Texas who collapse in heat waves that push past the limits of the human body, in the Vermont towns washed away by summer floods.
You saw it in COVID-19, too, when the poorest and most vulnerable died at staggering rates while leaders shrugged or denied the crisis altogether.
The pattern is painfully clear: the warnings ignored, the inequality magnified, the lives lost, and then the silence--the familiar silence filled with promises of resilience, rebuilding, and ribbon-cuttings in a future that never came.
So what exactly are we marking, twenty years on?
Not only the memory of those who were lost, but the truth that we squandered in the moment.
Katrina taught us what happens when a powerful storm collides with a fragile society.
The tragedy is that, two decades later, we are still choosing fragility.
The most dangerous flood walls in this country are not the levees around New Orleans, but the walls of indifference and denial we keep building higher.
Climate change is not a future we're trying to prevent, but a present we're already living.
Katrina was the first chapter of that present.
And as I look back, as someone who witnessed the storm and the failures that followed, I know the question we face now is whether we will keep rereading that chapter--storm after storm, failure after failure--or finally decide to turn the page.
[Subscribed](https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubWVzb3NjYWxlbmV3cy5jb20vYWNjb3VudCIsInAiOjE3MTY4MDg2NSwicyI6MjgzNDQ5LCJmIjpmYWxzZSwidSI6OTA1MDExOTIsImlhdCI6MTc1NTg4ODcwMSwiZXhwIjoyMDcxNDY0NzAxLCJpc3MiOiJwdWItMCIsInN1YiI6ImxpbmstcmVkaXJlY3QifQ.AwolHLsI-I4BDiBPsDv__yYp94X34SMmZaxpAnk-6Z4?)

clean_body: ##August 29, 2005 -- Hurricane Katrina makes landfall | | ---|---|--- | | | Forwarded this email? ? for more ? Thank you for being a paid subscriber! You can upgrade to Founder level to help bring this important work to more readers! ? Aug 22 | | | | | | | | | | | ##August 29, 2005 -- Hurricane Katrina makes landfall As we approach the 20th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina, I'll be publishing several pieces covering my own perspective, the challenges still creating danger in South Louisiana, and lessons learned (and the ones that weren't). Please consider upgrading to a paid subscription so I can keep this newsletter free to the world. ? Very few moments in a lifetime actually shift the axis your world spins on. For me, Katrina was one of them--the storm that rewired how I saw the world, matched only by the day my son was born and when the state raided my home. What I lived through on August 29, 2005, and in the long, bitter weeks that followed, planted in me the conviction that truth is not optional, that someone has to hold it fast. That storm was less a detour than a compass, and it set me on the path I still walk today. It's hard to wrap my head around the fact that twenty years have passed since the storm. Time says it's history, but it doesn't feel that way--it feels like something still raw and open, a wound that never closed. Katrina didn't just arrive and vanish; it settled in, reshaping lives, communities, the whole country. I was there, and in many ways, I'm still there. The storm lingers, like high water that never quite drains away. What Katrina revealed was not simply the power of a hurricane, but the fragility of a society that had ignored every warning. I remember reading the maps years before--New Orleans drawn like a bowl, with walls of dirt between it and the sea, everyone knowing those levees were not built to withstand what the Gulf could send. Engineers and scientists said it plainly. When the storm came, it did what we knew it would. The failure wasn't natural. It was political. It was social. And after the waters finally receded, I thought, surely this was the moment the country would wake up. Surely, after watching our fellow citizens stranded on rooftops and left to die in the heat of the Superdome, we would treat poverty and climate as the national emergencies they are. But we didn't. We went back to business as usual. We let leaders call it a freak storm, an anomaly. We convinced ourselves it wouldn't happen again. South Louisiana still bears the scars. I've walked through neighborhoods where the houses remain gutted, skeletal reminders of promises never kept. I've spoken with families who fled to Houston, Atlanta, Dallas--who wanted to come home but never could. Those who did return often found their communities reshaped not by renewal but by removal. Entire blocks of Black and working-class families were erased, replaced by developers and investors. I can still see the waterlines, faint and high up the walls, as if the city itself keeps a memory of its drowning. And then the storms kept coming--Rita just weeks later, then Gustav, Ike, Isaac, Ida. ? Each one another hammer on a glass wall already cracked. Every hundred minutes, Louisiana loses a football field of land to the Gulf, wetlands slashed apart by oil and gas canals, the sea reclaiming what human neglect had left defenseless. But if Louisiana bore the brunt of the levee failures, Mississippi absorbed the storm itself. The surge there was almost unthinkable -- 28.5 feet high in some places, turning coastal towns into kindling. I drove through Waveland and Bay St. Louis months later, and it looked as if someone had scraped the coastline clean with a giant blade. Whole neighborhoods gone, not just damaged but erased. Casinos that had once floated just offshore were hurled inland like toys, crushing houses beneath them. Families who had survived generations of hurricanes -- 1969's Camille included --stood stunned in front of nothing but slabs. Biloxi's historic waterfront was shredded, its shrimping fleets splintered, its homes flattened. It's telling that even today, many of those Mississippi towns never truly came back; the tax base was too weak, the resources too few, and the national attention too fixated on New Orleans to sustain long-term rebuilding. Mississippi's pain was quieter, less televised, but no less immense. And I think often about how little has changed in the systems meant to protect us. FEMA is still a maze of forms and delays, quicker to redraw insurance maps than to deliver aid. Emergency response still bends toward those with means. And most damning of all, the carbon keeps pouring into the atmosphere. In the twenty years since Katrina, we've emitted more greenhouse gases than in all of human history before 1990. We knew -- we knew -- that hotter seas meant stronger hurricanes, that warmer air meant wetter storms, that rising seas meant higher surges. We had the science. We had the evidence. And still we chose to accelerate, to subsidize fossil fuels, to silence the very warnings that might have spared lives. The media didn't help. ? I remember watching how they framed it: Black families wading through chest-deep water in search of food and medicine were called "looters." White families doing the same were called "survivors." Reporters descended to marvel at the spectacle of misery but too rarely interrogated the policies that made it inevitable. Mississippi, especially, slipped out of the narrative almost entirely. The images from New Orleans--the helicopters, the Superdome, the Convention Center--sucked up all the airtime, while the coast to the east was left largely invisible. It was as if an entire state's devastation didn't count because it didn't fit the storyline. That failure hasn't disappeared. To this day, most storm coverage is still framed in radar loops and wind speeds, reporters lashed to poles, drama without depth. Rarely do they connect the dots: poverty makes disasters deadlier, climate change makes them stronger, policy makes them inevitable. Katrina became a symbol of tragedy, but not the catalyst for the kind of transformation it demanded. And if you look around America in 2025, you see Katrina everywhere. You see it in the wildfires that turn California's skies into a kind of apocalyptic red, in the farmworkers in Texas who collapse in heat waves that push past the limits of the human body, in the Vermont towns washed away by summer floods. You saw it in COVID-19, too, when the poorest and most vulnerable died at staggering rates while leaders shrugged or denied the crisis altogether. The pattern is painfully clear: the warnings ignored, the inequality magnified, the lives lost, and then the silence--the familiar silence filled with promises of resilience, rebuilding, and ribbon-cuttings in a future that never came. So what exactly are we marking, twenty years on? Not only the memory of those who were lost, but the truth that we squandered in the moment. Katrina taught us what happens when a powerful storm collides with a fragile society. The tragedy is that, two decades later, we are still choosing fragility. The most dangerous flood walls in this country are not the levees around New Orleans, but the walls of indifference and denial we keep building higher. Climate change is not a future we're trying to prevent, but a present we're already living. Katrina was the first chapter of that present. And as I look back, as someone who witnessed the storm and the failures that followed, I know the question we face now is whether we will keep rereading that chapter--storm after storm, failure after failure--or finally decide to turn the page. ? I’m reaching out because I needed to show you this: [](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113809926/565012650/1506335976?refcode=email_082225_mailvoting&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTAvMS83MjY1OCIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJiZTM4MmJhYS04ODdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=gbpno5VVe5D_CsTqz4LJU9KAW575tXPn7bOk_tgQa5A=) **Donald Trump just called mail-in voting “corrupt,” and threatened to issue an executive order banning it entirely throughout the nation.** This is completely ludicrous, and clearly unconstitutional. It’s been proven time and again that voting by mail is easy, secure, saves money, and most importantly, _it helps ensure more people are able to make their voices heard on Election Day._ And Coloradans would know — **we launched Colorado's universal vote by mail system back when I was governor in 2013.** We pioneered Colorado’s gold-standard for free and fair elections because making it easier for people to have a say in our democracy is the right thing to do. In Colorado, every voter gets a mail-in ballot, and we're consistently one of the top states for voter turnout. That only proves we should be making it easier for everyday folks to vote, not harder! The bottom line is, Trump needs to stop messing with our elections. It’s simply un-American. **And we’ll stand up to it every step of the way.** **[So can you pitch in a couple bucks to help us fight back? Now , this regime is going after cities and lawmakers they view as political enemies—going so far as to send National Guard troops to Los Angeles and Washington, D.C., and to coerce Democratic lawmakers in Texas with financial penalties and police surveillance.
They’re testing the waters to see what they can get away with. If we don’t resist and lay the groundwork to elect leaders like Maxine Dexter— people who aren’t afraid to take on Trump and his cronies —we’ll see them erode our rights until we have no recourse.
Can we count on you to stand with us as we build a people-powered resistance in the House? [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/upl_med_email-25q3?refcode=x_250823_EOM1_act]

clean_body: [] Hey Peter, The modern Republican Party, their megadonors, and MAGA extremists think of themselves as above the law, especially when it comes to their Criminal-in-Chief, Donald Trump—convicted felon and former associate of Jeffrey Epstein. Here’s Dr. Maxine Dexter’s diagnosis: our country is being governed by authoritarians. They don’t believe that all Americans are created equal or deserve the same rights. And they’re determined to chip away at our freedoms to hold onto power. Next November, we have a chance to stop these anti-American, anti-Constitution extremists in their tracks by taking back the House and Senate. Will you rush what you can today to help fuel our fight? [] FUEL THE FIGHT [] First , Trump and the GOP slashed the federal workforce—culling the people who acted as a system of checks and balances. In his first ten days alone , Trump fired more than a dozen lawyers at the Department of Justice who worked on cases against him . Then , these MAGA extremists empowered ICE to kidnap people off the street, falsely detaining individuals who are in our country legally, and violating the constitutionally protected right to due process. Now , this regime is going after cities and lawmakers they view as political enemies—going so far as to send National Guard troops to Los Angeles and Washington, D.C., and to coerce Democratic lawmakers in Texas with financial penalties and police surveillance. They’re testing the waters to see what they can get away with. If we don’t resist and lay the groundwork to elect leaders like Maxine Dexter— people who aren’t afraid to take on Trump and his cronies —we’ll see them erode our rights until we have no recourse. Can we count on you to stand with us as we build a people-powered resistance in the House? [] If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will go through immediately: DONATE $5 [] DONATE $10 [] DONATE $25 [] DONATE $50 [] DONATE $100 [] OTHER [] — Dexter HQ [] Maxine Dexter is a critical care physician with decades of experience using facts and following science to help her patients. Now she's using those skills in Congress to ensure healthy communities by standing up to Big Pharma, fighting climate change, and expanding reproductive rights, as well as rebuilding a fair economy for all by protecting Social Security and Medicare. To learn more about Maxine, visit MaxineForOregon.com [] . name: Rev. Raphael Warnock
subject: Trump’s new attacks on voting rights
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Warnock for Georgia
body:
| | |
---
Friend, I’m writing with an important update on voting rights.
As you may have heard, Donald Trump has been calling GOP governors and state legislators asking them to draw new, gerrymandered districts in their states – midway through the decade – in a brazen attempt to steal congressional seats and bolster his Republican majority.
**Now, he’s taken another drastic step: calling for an end to _all_ mail-in voting.**
Look, I’ve spent my whole career fighting for voting rights. I’m not about to let Donald Trump take away the voices of the people without a fight, and I know you aren’t either.
That’s why I’m counting on you to take action today and sign my brand-new petition to protect voting rights.**[Please, if you’re with me, will you add your name now to condemn Trump’s voter suppression efforts and protect the right to vote?](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113810905/565015384/1716918780?email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&zip=18326&ask=5&amounts=250,100,50,25,5&code=jtk5586-su-nd&refcode=jtk5586-su-nd&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNTEvMS85MDkwMSIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIwZTM0NTczMS04YTdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=GNfymcaOug9voRXojgoIToWCfmL0OV1gffmZOHQJdnw=&emci=3cca9040-917e-f011-b484-6045bdeb7413&emdi=0e345731-8a7f-f011-b484-6045bdeb7413&ceid=1586379)**
| | [Sign the Petition](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113810907/565015385/1716918780?email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&zip=18326&ask=5&amounts=250,100,50,25,5&code=jtk5586-su-nd&refcode=jtk5586-su-nd&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNTEvMS85MDkwMSIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIwZTM0NTczMS04YTdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=GNfymcaOug9voRXojgoIToWCfmL0OV1gffmZOHQJdnw=&emci=3cca9040-917e-f011-b484-6045bdeb7413&emdi=0e345731-8a7f-f011-b484-6045bdeb7413&ceid=1586379)
---
My question for Donald Trump is this: What are you so afraid of? Are you afraid that the people might _actually_ hold you accountable at the ballot box?
It sure seems to me like Trump knows his policies are unpopular and causing pain in communities all across our country. But instead of changing his policies, he’s trying to change the rules – and silence your voice.
That’s why I’m calling out his brazen attempts to undermine voting rights, and I’m grateful to you for joining me. Together, I know we can win this fight and protect the country we love.
Thanks for speaking out,
Rev. Raphael Warnock
| This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com.
[To unsubscribe from this email list, please click here.](https://action.warnockforgeorgia.com/p/UBjz5MzSPkqVKiEwLzz2KA2?unsubscribedata=Mkk4xu%2ByLuMBrnCvAbhZLMsoASxR27WwO9bHTCYngnA9w%2BxVPV5ptBzdLMMslkqNFhBUgE2Q6x1bQ9AHzeoPtv1tqQWkRKBsWrYUqnYwuPfzqYnXoZXAuiD2xIHnRLEoTuMM0QuKccCaDeHGiCN4sdKC2cYAM%2BxwOa5ChvGsFs0dPg6zkSS0MxRSIfhExr2QSehRQDNxGmt1wTbq1tg8UNIm4IPxMjh5cvaNogPysXUO5nacbrrYZyHrizk50WOhPHuchqZELwjYXAqdsv0EZmYt8n8w5Od0f4pLmIN%2BrolciD0JqZdpMUhwyuxluF%2F5xI02KxaGiQGySRZXzy4419pu8wPVo%2BjMDdes1zImQ37RflH9rkDF8eaJ0HU4C6xnf41WeLKVuIePJhr0WlcRx1A0Vt1HWnUS%2FdHekJbkr1U%3D) For your reference, any donor history or identification in this message reflects what we have on file for the email address [dpwillis67@gmail.com] to which this message was sent. Friend,

I promise I won't take up much of your time.

In my film _2000 Mules_ , I exposed one of the greatest scandals in American history - the hijacking of the 2020 presidential election.

Today, I'm proud to join forces with Stand in the Gap, a non-profit organization dedicated to supporting the patriots who exercised their First Amendment rights in protest of the _outcome_ of that election.

**I want to reach as many people as I can with this request...**

I told their founder, Shane Jenkins, that I would help him raise $20,000 before the end of the month to help Stand in the Gap provide help, hope, and healing to the January 6th patriots.

clean_body: Friend, I promise I won't take up much of your time. In my film 2000 Mules , I exposed one of the greatest scandals in American history - the hijacking of the 2020 presidential election. Today, I'm proud to join forces with Stand in the Gap, a non-profit organization dedicated to supporting the patriots who exercised their First Amendment rights in protest of the outcome of that election. I want to reach as many people as I can with this request... I told their founder, Shane Jenkins, that I would help him raise $20,000 before the end of the month to help Stand in the Gap provide help, hope, and healing to the January 6th patriots. Even a donation of $10 can give a January 6th patriot the support they need. Otherwise, let me explain why I 'm asking you to donate at least $10: $10 because the " crime" these patriots committed was fighting for their country. $10 because even though they've been pardoned, many of them are struggling to get back on their feet. $10 because Stand in the Gap is providing them with the resources they need to successfully reintegrate into their communities, find work, reconnect with family, and restore their rights. Not only are these men and women patriots, but they come from all walks of life -- law enforcement, veterans, white-collar professionals, blue-collar workers, and all races and religions. And they were hunted down in a political persecution that denied them their constitutional rights. Stand in the Gap exists to provide real, practical help, like rent money, job training, transportation, legal referrals, trauma counseling, and health insurance, to help these persecuted patriots move on with their lives. Your gift today can help one of these patriots find the help, hope, and healing they need to succeed in their second chance. Friend, can I count on you to contribute $10 or whatever you can afford to Stand in the Gap? Click any button below to give today. Thank you, Dinesh D'Souza P.S. If you're still reading this email and I haven't convinced you to donate $10 or more, please consider what January 6th patriots still face... Harassment on social media and in their personal lives Difficulties in finding a job Homelessness Loss of rights, like the right to vote or own a firearm * Mental health challenges that their families can never understand All they did was stand up for their beliefs. They do not deserve this sort of treatment. But for just $10, you can help Stand in the Gap give pardoned January 6th patriots the support they need to succeed at their second chance. Stand In The Gap is registered as a 501(c)(3) non-profit organization. All contributions are tax-deductible to the extent permitted by law. Stand In The Gap's tax identification number is 93-1417599.

Peter, during her 2024 campaign, Mariannette Miller-Meeks ran an ad that promised Iowans lower grocery prices if she won the election.
[](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/113811664/565017619/-1612730695?refcode=em25_250822_grocer_fnd_gq_act___TB_ettx&utm_campaign=em25_250822_grocer_fnd_gq_act___TB_ettx&utm_medium=email&refcode2=1a33bd54-8b7f-f011-b484-6045bdeb7413&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTIvMS85MjIyOCIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIxYTMzYmQ1NC04YjdmLWYwMTEtYjQ4NC02MDQ1YmRlYjc0MTMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=7wyonxsZB-6HSJ_jM1cgec1vP_96RPR-Y2JNfbIOnsk=)
But today, prices are at an all-time high. **Food overall has gone up more than 3%, and the cost of fresh and dry vegetables has skyrocketed nearly 40%. Meat, poultry, eggs, and coffee — the staples that every family depends on — are stretching household budgets past the breaking point.**

While Iowans are squeezed tighter every week, Miller-Meeks voted to hand out tax breaks to the wealthy and supported tariffs that raise prices even more. _She chose the wealthy and well-connected, not Iowans standing in the checkout line._

Christina knows Iowans are tired of politicians making empty promises. She knows nothing means more than true action, and she’s ready to fight for this district with everything she’s got. In Congress, she’ll work every day to bring costs down and give families some breathing room. **[She is the candidate for your wallet, and with your support we can send her to Washington in 2026 to fight for you. Food overall has gone up more than 3%, and the cost of fresh and dry vegetables has skyrocketed nearly 40%. Meat, poultry, eggs, and coffee — the staples that every family depends on — are stretching household budgets past the breaking point. While Iowans are squeezed tighter every week, Miller-Meeks voted to hand out tax breaks to the wealthy and supported tariffs that raise prices even more. She chose the wealthy and well-connected, not Iowans standing in the checkout line. Christina knows Iowans are tired of politicians making empty promises. She knows nothing means more than true action, and she’s ready to fight for this district with everything she’s got. In Congress, she’ll work every day to bring costs down and give families some breathing room. = If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will go through name: Dana Criswell
subject: A Few Straight Answers to Your Top Bitcoin Questions
body:
After I published "Bitcoin: Sound Money for a Free Society", many of you sent thoughtful questions and pushback. "Real money" is whatever people accept that reliably settles transactions. Bitcoin does that globally, 24/7. And no, you can't "just create more."

| [](https://substack.com/redirect/9773bdb8-f755-4588-b9c6-934dbb061534?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
Photo by [Kanchanara](https://substack.com/redirect/c8455542-df8c-4ae1-8f35-41e0f5e972be?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) on [Unsplash](https://substack.com/redirect/19dee276-c878-465e-8b3d-d120c115d192?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
After I published "Bitcoin: Sound Money for a Free Society", many of you sent thoughtful questions and pushback. This brief follow-up is meant to address some of those comments. My aim is to clarify how Bitcoin actually works and why those mechanics matter for everyday savers, small businesses, and citizens who care about economic freedom. I've included some links to sources where these answers were found.
## "If the grid goes down, Bitcoin is worthless. Paper cash still works."
Short outages favor local cash--agreed. But most dollars today aren't paper; they're database entries at banks and card networks that also depend on power and the internet. Bitcoin can add redundancy: you can _receive_ the blockchain by satellite (no internet) and there are experimental ways to _send_ transactions using radio, mesh networks, or even basic phones via USSD/SMS (used in parts of Africa). None of these are silver bullets, but they show Bitcoin isn't a one-wire system. Cash for local resilience, Bitcoin for censorship-resistant settlement--they complement each other. [Blockstream](https://substack.com/redirect/a6103d0a-18f4-40d1-b1ab-9f0a06b91d76?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) \- [Bitcoin Magazine](https://substack.com/redirect/28cfaaae-0bc1-4e33-9975-b206a17fc7df?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) \- [CoinDesk](https://substack.com/redirect/278af78d-25ef-4cde-8ac2-41c326482863?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
## "Florida (and others) banned this 'ghost dollar.'"
Not so. Florida passed laws **against a central bank digital currency (CBDC)** --government money with programmable controls--not against Bitcoin. Separately, several states and cities are tightening rules or even banning some **crypto ATMs** to combat fraud, which is about kiosk regulation, not outlawing Bitcoin itself. [Florida Governor's Office](https://substack.com/redirect/4e700628-4560-414d-aca7-d6586c5d2800?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) \- [The Florida Senate](https://substack.com/redirect/1923e5ee-08c5-4809-86cd-804777dfd3b1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) \- [Axios+1](https://substack.com/redirect/63d8252d-c71e-4c45-a8ba-dda9f72e5f59?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
## "Government can track and freeze every penny."
Government can regulate **companies** (exchanges, banks) and subpoena records. On-chain, Bitcoin is **pseudonymous** : transactions are public, identities are not--until you attach your name at an on-ramp. Blockchain forensics can and does trace crime; that's a feature of a transparent ledger, not a flaw. If you self-custody, no one can arbitrarily print more of your money or seize it without your keys and due process--though you also take on the responsibility to secure it. [Investopedia](https://substack.com/redirect/53a373dc-3990-4edc-96c8-77dec95c2da9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) \- [Chainalysis](https://substack.com/redirect/cbe9dacd-6e37-4316-9a3b-e9bb55ca8322?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) \- [WIRED](https://substack.com/redirect/e1577db9-0efb-4e82-a89b-dc0a75435c09?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
## "Bitcoin isn't real money, and anyone can create it."
"Real money" is whatever people accept that reliably settles transactions. Bitcoin does that globally, 24/7. And **no** , you can't "just create more." The protocol's supply is hard-capped at 21 million, enforced by thousands of independent nodes that reject any invalid block. You can _try_ to copy the code, but you can't clone the network's users, miners, or liquidity--social consensus is the moat. (That's also why governments can't change Bitcoin's issuance schedule.) [Investopedia](https://substack.com/redirect/eb9742fe-1ab6-45a4-8b8e-a9a4f3406f40?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
## "What about fraud and seniors being scammed?"
This is a real problem--especially with crypto kiosks. States are responding with limits, warnings, and licensing. Consumer education matters too: never pay a "government fee" or "urgent bill" via a kiosk; verify first; and favor reputable on-ramps. Regulation aimed at scams can coexist with open, neutral money. [MediaRoom](https://substack.com/redirect/f878912e-76f6-4ba1-9d6a-fe12917fea38?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) \- [Midland Daily News](https://substack.com/redirect/806ffd60-c606-4a51-b752-aa678608bc80?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
## Bottom line
Fiat is money the state can expand and censor; Bitcoin is money with rules that **don 't change by decree**. That doesn't make it magic or risk-free. It means we finally have a monetary system whose core properties--scarcity, openness, and settlement--don't depend on trusting politicians or corporate gatekeepers. Use cash when it's practical, banks when it's convenient, and Bitcoin when sovereignty and portability matter most.
I'd recommend checking out [www.JoelBomgar.com](https://substack.com/redirect/7b99e16b-3b89-4e88-a4f2-1994efc8f97a?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). Joel has a wealth of information about Bitcoin.
**Read all of Dana 's post and stay informed about politics in Mississippi**

My aim is to clarify how Bitcoin actually works and why those mechanics matter for everyday savers, small businesses, and citizens who care about economic freedom. I've included some links to sources where these answers were found. "If the grid goes down, Bitcoin is worthless. Paper cash still works." Short outages favor local cash--agreed. But most dollars today aren't paper; they're database entries at banks and card networks that also depend on power and the internet. Bitcoin can add redundancy: you can receive the blockchain by satellite (no internet) and there are experimental ways to send transactions using radio, mesh networks, or even basic phones via USSD/SMS (used in parts of Africa). None of these are silver bullets, but they show Bitcoin isn't a one-wire system. Cash for local resilience, Bitcoin for censorship-resistant settlement--they complement each other. \- \- "Florida (and others) banned this 'ghost dollar.'" Not so. Florida passed laws against a central bank digital currency (CBDC) --government money with programmable controls--not against Bitcoin. Separately, several states and cities are tightening rules or even banning some crypto ATMs to combat fraud, which is about kiosk regulation, not outlawing Bitcoin itself. \- \- "Government can track and freeze every penny." body:
